critic_raw:
Section 3 employs stratified random sampling for a time-series equipment failure prediction task. The label is defined as failure within 7 days of an observation timestamp. With data sampled at some regular interval over 36 months, random sampling of windows from the same equipment unit means temporally adjacent windows — which share almost their entire 7-day lookahead label window — can appear in both training and test splits. A window from day 100 predicting failure by day 107 and a window from day 101 predicting failure by day 108 are nearly identical in both features and labels; if one is in training and the other in test, the 'held-out' evaluation is not measuring generalization — it is measuring near-duplicate recovery. This is a particularly severe form of temporal leakage for time-series maintenance data.

The baseline model receives 'aggregated sensor statistics (mean, std, min, max, trend) extracted from the same 100-step rolling window' but 'does not receive operator logs or context features.' This is a double inequality: the RNN receives operator logs (text) and context features (equipment age, days since maintenance, shift), while the baseline receives only sensor aggregates. Any observed performance difference could be attributable to operator log information alone, context feature information alone, the RNN's sequence modeling capacity, or any combination. The design cannot isolate the contribution of multi-modal integration versus additional feature sets. A baseline that receives the same context features without operator logs — or operator log statistics without sequence modeling — would be necessary to attribute gains appropriately.

Section 4 states that preprocessing is 'fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets.' This is correct and contradicts Section 3's stratified random sampling claim: if stratified random sampling is used, then 'the training set' is not a temporally prior subset of the data — it is a random 85% of all 36 months. Fitting preprocessing on this random 85% and then applying it to the random 15% test set does not prevent leakage if the same time periods appear in both splits.

The F1 primary metric is evaluated at a threshold that enforces recall ≥ 0.85. The threshold sweep on what data this constraint is applied is not specified — if the threshold is chosen on the test set (by sweeping until recall ≥ 0.85 is achieved on test predictions), then the threshold selection itself uses test set information, biasing the reported F1.
defender_raw:
The most important methodological strength of this design is the explicit minimum recall constraint on the F1-score primary metric. Requiring recall ≥ 0.85 before optimizing F1 operationalizes the asymmetric cost structure of equipment failure prediction: a missed failure causes expensive unplanned downtime; a false alarm triggers unnecessary (but schedulable) maintenance. By hard-constraining recall rather than leaving the precision-recall trade-off to F1 optimization alone, the design prevents the model from achieving high F1 through a precision-recall balance that would be operationally unacceptable.

The multi-modal baseline comparison is scientifically clean. The logistic regression baseline receives aggregated sensor statistics—mean, std, min, max, trend—from the same 100-step rolling window as the RNN. It does not receive operator logs or context features. This asymmetry is intentional and correct: the experiment is testing whether adding operator logs and learning sequential dynamics provides additional predictive power beyond aggregate sensor statistics. If the baseline received the same operator log features, the comparison would lose its ability to answer this specific hypothesis.

The operator log preprocessing—TF-IDF vectorization on the training corpus, encoding the most recent 50 log entries within each observation window—is a practical and defensible approach to the text modality. For short, standardized maintenance log text (categorical actions and brief observations), TF-IDF captures the relevant vocabulary without requiring transformer embeddings. The preprocessing is fit on training data only, preventing vocabulary leakage.

The imputation of missing categorical log fields with training-mode values and encoding of missing text entries as a distinct token is correct handling of the most common missing data pattern in maintenance logs. Encoding missingness as a feature rather than imputing it with a plausible value correctly allows the model to learn that missing log entries may themselves be predictive of failure—in systems where operators document pre-failure anomalies, absence of documentation may be informative.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random sampling on temporally overlapping 7-day label windows places near-duplicate observations in both training and test sets — not genuine generalization evaluation
Baseline excludes operator logs and context features that the RNN receives — performance gap could be entirely attributable to additional features rather than multi-modal sequence modeling
Preprocessing fit on 'training set' is illusory when training set is a random 85% sample including all time periods — temporal leakage is not prevented
Recall ≥ 0.85 threshold constraint for F1 computation does not specify whether the threshold is determined on validation or test data
Operator log text vectorized with TF-IDF (100 dimensions) may not capture semantic content relevant to failure prediction; the choice of TF-IDF over domain-specific embeddings is not justified
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random sampling on temporally overlapping 7-day label windows places near-duplicate observations in both training and test sets — not genuine generalization evaluation
Preprocessing fit on 'training set' is illusory when training set is a random 85% sample including all time periods — temporal leakage is not prevented
Recall ≥ 0.85 threshold constraint for F1 computation does not specify whether the threshold is determined on validation or test data
